ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for genmaicha (brown rice green tea). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is green tea beverage (FOODON_03311737); genmaicha is a subtype blending green tea with roasted brown rice.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Green tea, infusion) is the closest defensible match. Genmaicha is a blended green tea with roasted brown rice; no dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for this specific blend.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for genmaicha or brown rice tea as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed genmaicha (approximately 240 ml) is the typical single serving for this unsweetened beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, green, brewed, prepared with tap water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14355); supplemented by Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for Brown Rice Tea (no sugar) (D09020017)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 9 kcal, protein 0.12 g, fat 0.02 g, carbohydrate 1.9 g, sugar 0.0 g, dietary fibre 0.04 g) are taken directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo record D09020017 which is the primary approved source for this Singapore-catalogued beverage. Sodium is reported as -1 in the HPB source (data not available); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for brewed green tea (NDB 14355) reports sodium at approximately 7 mg/100 ml and is used as a secondary reference. Saturated fat, calcium, and iron are confirmed at 0.0 g / 0.0 mg / 0.0 mg per HPB. Remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of beverages and tea infusions — analogous estimate based on unsweetened green tea (GI ~15) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for genmaicha (brown rice tea, no sugar) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on unsweetened green tea infusion, which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Genmaicha shares the same base (green tea infusion) with negligible additional carbohydrate from roasted brown rice. Glycemic load per 240 ml serving = (15 × 1.9 × 2.4) / 100 = 0.68, rounded to 0.3 per 100 g basis (GL = GI × carbohydrate_g / 100 = 15 × 1.9 / 100 = 0.285 ≈ 0.3 per 100 g). The original input GI value of 55 from 'Embedded GI Database' is not an approved source and has been replaced.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium; sodium data not available from HPB source but USDA reference for brewed green tea indicates approximately 7 mg/100 ml; no added salt or sodium-raising ingredients
hypertension.guidance: Brown rice tea (genmaicha, no sugar) is a very low-calorie, effectively sodium-free beverage that is safe and appropriate for individuals managing hypertension; green tea polyphenols (catechins) have demonstrated mild vasodilatory and blood-pressure-lowering effects in clinical studies, and caffeine content is low, though those with caffeine sensitivity should moderate intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero added sugar; negligible carbohydrate (1.9 g/100 ml); low GI (~15); green tea catechins support insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brown rice tea (genmaicha, no sugar) has a very low glycemic index (~15) and negligible carbohydrate load, producing no meaningful postprandial glucose rise; green tea catechins (EGCG) have evidence-based benefits for improving insulin sensitivity, making this beverage a clinically appropriate choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided no sugar or sweeteners are added.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat; zero cholesterol; green tea polyphenols with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brown rice tea (genmaicha, no sugar) contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and no added sugar, and the green tea polyphenol content (catechins, EGCG) is associated with reduced LDL oxidation and improved endothelial function, making it a heart-healthy beverage choice for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Green tea catechins and polyphenols with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Genmaicha contains green tea catechins and polyphenols which are evidence-based anti-inflammatory compounds; zero sodium, zero fat, and zero sugar mean no pro-inflammatory components, making this a suitable beverage for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, though caffeine should be monitored if on RA medications with caffeine interactions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown rice tea (no sugar), known in Japanese as genmaicha (玄米茶), is a traditional Japanese blended tea made by combining sencha or bancha green tea leaves with roasted brown rice kernels, some of which may have popped during roasting to resemble popcorn. The beverage is brewed as a hot or cold infusion and consumed without any added sugar or sweeteners. Genmaicha has a distinctive nutty, toasty flavour from the roasted rice, balanced by the grassy, vegetal notes of green tea, and a light golden-green colour. The texture is that of a clear, light-bodied tea infusion. Per 100 ml, it provides only 9 kcal, 0.12 g protein, 0.02 g fat, 1.9 g carbohydrate, 0.0 g sugar, and 0.04 g dietary fibre, making it one of the lowest-calorie beverages available. Sodium data is not available from the primary HPB source. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), based on analogy with unsweetened green tea infusion, and the glycemic load per serving is negligible. Clinically, genmaicha is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (no sugar, low GI, catechins support insulin sensitivity), hypertension (effectively sodium-free, polyphenols may support vasodilation), and cardiovascular disease (no saturated fat, no cholesterol, antioxidant polyphenols). It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Genmaicha is widely consumed in Japan as an everyday tea and is increasingly popular in Singapore and internationally as a health-conscious beverage choice.
common_user_queries: Is brown rice tea good for diabetes?
Does brown rice tea have sugar?
Is brown rice tea suitable for hypertension?
What does brown rice tea taste like?
Is genmaicha the same as green tea?
Can I drink genmaicha on a keto diet?

confidence_reason: Core macros are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (D09020017), which is an approved primary source for this Singapore-catalogued beverage. The food identity is well-established. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using unsweetened green tea as the closest approved equivalent, which is clearly documented. Multilingual Japanese naming is confirmed. Confidence is high for macros and medium for GI estimate; overall record confidence is high.
notes: 1. sodium_mg is set to -1: the Singapore HPB Foodo source (D09020017) does not report a sodium value for this beverage (sentinel value -1 in input). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14355, brewed green tea) reports approximately 7 mg sodium per 100 ml as a secondary reference, but this value has not been adopted into per_100g as the primary HPB source takes precedence and reports data as unavailable. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for genmaicha are not available in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a directly matching entry. 3. The input GI value of 55 (medium) from 'Embedded GI Database' has been replaced; 'Embedded GI Database' is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2. The replacement GI of 15 (low) is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using unsweetened green tea infusion as the closest approved equivalent. 4. calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed at 0.0 per HPB source. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (no added sugar, no naturally occurring sugar in the infusion). saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero given fat_g = 0.02 g total fat with no saturated fat reported. 5. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been preserved in llm_training meal context as this beverage is compatible with ketogenic diets given negligible carbohydrate content.
